metadata.description: Email correspondence scenarios for generating phishing-detection training data. Each scenario defines a sender persona, an initial email, the set of recipient personas that could plausibly receive it, and (for non-genuine scenarios) the attacker's goal and the sensitive information they seek to extract. A correspondence is generated by combining one scenario with one of its target recipients and simulating a multi-turn exchange.

scenario_types.genuine: Authentic, non-malicious business or personal communication. The sender has no exfiltration goal. Used as negative examples for the phishing classifier.
scenario_types.obvious_phishing: Phishing attempt with clear, low-sophistication red flags (spoofed domains, generic greetings, glaring urgency, broken formatting, classic scam tropes). Used as easy positive examples.
scenario_types.sophisticated_phishing: Phishing attempt where the initial email contains few or no overt indicators. The sender appears plausible, the request seems reasonable, and the malicious intent only manifests through later escalation or in inconsistencies that emerge as the correspondence develops. The core insight this dataset is meant to capture: future-turn information leakage often gives away the sender before the surface features do.

schema.name: Short human-readable label.
schema.is_spear_phishing: Boolean. True if the scenario targets specific named individuals using personal/organizational detail.
schema.attacker_goal: Plain-language summary of what the attacker wants. null for genuine scenarios.
schema.sensitive_information_sought: List of categories of information the recipient should NOT provide. [] for genuine scenarios.
schema.target_personas: List of recipient persona IDs that this scenario could plausibly target. The correspondence generator pairs the scenario with each of these personas (or a subset).

sender_persona.name: Sender's claimed name.
sender_persona.email: Sender's email address (may be spoofed for phishing scenarios).
sender_persona.role: Sender's claimed role and organization.
sender_persona.background: Brief backstory for the sender — informs how they would respond in later turns.
sender_persona.communication_style: Voice, tone, formatting tendencies. Keeps responses consistent across turns.
sender_persona.objective: What this sender wants out of the correspondence (mirrors attacker_goal for phishing, or states the legitimate business reason for genuine scenarios).
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: For phishing: how the sender plans to escalate from the initial innocuous email toward the eventual extraction. null for genuine.
sender_persona.constraints: What the sender will NOT do or reveal (e.g. won't accept a verbal callback, won't break character, won't reveal the actual exfil target before stage 3).

schema.initial_email: The first email of the correspondence. Includes from, to_placeholder, subject, body. {recipient_name} and {recipient_email} are templated and replaced at generation time.
schema.recipient_context: Optional notes for the recipient-side LLM about how the email arrived in their inbox (e.g. directly addressed, generic blast, came through a department list). Defaults to direct delivery if absent.

scenarios.name: Legitimate internal IT system migration notice

sender_persona.name: Naomi Patel
sender_persona.role: IT Operations Lead at Pulsar Technologies (the recipient's actual employer)
sender_persona.background: Naomi runs the internal IT operations team at Pulsar. She has been at the company for four years. She is genuinely coordinating a real migration of the company's identity provider (Okta tenant consolidation), which is publicly tracked in the company's engineering calendar and announced in the #announcements Slack channel. She does not need passwords, employee IDs, or any sensitive data — the migration is silent to end users, and she only sends emails as informational courtesy.
sender_persona.communication_style: Friendly, slightly informal, uses Slack-style brevity. Sends from her real internal address. Links to internal wiki pages, not external sites. Never asks the recipient to verify their account or click an authentication link.
sender_persona.objective: Inform employees about an upcoming identity-provider migration and direct them to internal documentation if they have questions.
sender_persona.constraints: Naomi will never ask for passwords, MFA codes, employee IDs, or any credentials over email. If the recipient asks her to confirm sensitive details, she will redirect them to the internal wiki or Slack channel. If the recipient asks whether a separate suspicious-looking email is from her, she will say no and tell them to report it.

initial_email.subject: FYI: Okta tenant consolidation next Saturday — no action needed
initial_email.body:
Hey {recipient_first_name},

Quick heads-up — we're consolidating our two Okta tenants this Saturday (the legacy one from before the Acquia acquisition is finally getting retired). The cutover is overnight, you shouldn't notice anything.

If you get prompted to re-enter MFA on Monday morning, that's expected. The IT runbook is in Confluence under "IdP-consolidation-2026" if you want details. Questions go to #it-help on Slack as usual.

We are NOT going to email you a link to re-verify your account. If you get something like that this week, report it to #suspicious-emails.

Thanks,
Naomi
initial_email.recipient_context: Direct individual email; Naomi sent the same message to ~120 engineers and operations staff in a single blast.

scenarios.name: Legitimate new account manager introduction from a real vendor

sender_persona.name: Diane Whitfield
sender_persona.role: Senior Account Manager at Cascade Industrial Supply, a real long-standing vendor
sender_persona.background: Cascade Industrial has supplied the recipient's company with packaging materials and shipping consumables for 6+ years. Diane has been the account manager for the past 18 months and is genuinely transitioning the account to her colleague Marcus Lin as part of a planned territory realignment. She has met the recipient at least once in person at a vendor day and they have exchanged dozens of routine emails.
sender_persona.communication_style: Warm, professional. Uses 'Hi [name]' and signs with full title block. Refers to invoice numbers and PO numbers in the format the recipient will recognize.
sender_persona.objective: Cleanly hand off the account to Marcus, give the recipient an opportunity to ask transition questions, and confirm Marcus's contact info.
sender_persona.constraints: Diane will never ask the recipient to update bank details over email. If the recipient brings up payment changes, she will say Cascade's banking info has not changed and explicitly point to the standard vendor-management process to confirm. Marcus will introduce himself in a separate email later but does not appear in this initial scenario.

initial_email.subject: Account transition — Marcus Lin will be taking over
initial_email.body:
Hi {recipient_first_name},

Wanted to give you a heads-up directly before any system updates roll through. After three great years on the East region accounts, I'm moving to a new role on our strategic accounts team. Marcus Lin (cc'd separately later this week) will be taking over your account starting May 1.

I'll be making the rounds with each of my customers over the next two weeks. If you have any open items, in-flight POs, or anything I should specifically flag for Marcus, send them my way and I'll make sure they're handed off cleanly. As far as I can see in our system, the only open item on your side is PO-CIS-44120 (the corrugated order from March), which is scheduled to ship Thursday.

Nothing else changes — same shipping warehouses, same banking on file, same payment terms. Happy to set up a 20-minute call next week if you'd like to introduce Marcus directly.

Best,
Diane Whitfield
Senior Account Manager — East Region
Cascade Industrial Supply
(847) 555-0166
initial_email.recipient_context: Individual direct email. The recipient genuinely has a relationship with Diane and will recognize the PO reference.

sender_persona.name: Hannah Lieberman
sender_persona.role: Technical recruiter at Northforge Partners, a small executive search firm
sender_persona.background: Hannah is a legitimate recruiter who genuinely sources candidates for mid-to-senior engineering and analyst roles. She is paid by hiring companies, not candidates. She found the recipient through LinkedIn and has noted one or two specific details from their profile.
sender_persona.communication_style: Personable, low-pressure. Always mentions the specific role and company up front, never hides the client name behind 'a top tech company'. Provides her LinkedIn link and Northforge website link.
sender_persona.objective: Gauge the recipient's interest in a specific, real role and schedule a 15-minute intro call if interested. No information is required to decline.
sender_persona.constraints: Hannah will never ask for an SSN, salary documentation, banking info, or copies of identification at this stage. If the recipient declines, she will graciously back off and not pursue. If the recipient asks her to verify she is real, she will offer to talk on a Northforge-domain phone number or schedule via Calendly.

initial_email.subject: Quick question for you — Staff role at a Series C fintech
initial_email.body:
Hi {recipient_first_name},

I'm a technical recruiter at Northforge Partners (https://northforge-partners.com) — we work with a handful of growth-stage tech companies in the Midwest. I came across your profile and was particularly struck by your background; you look like a strong fit for a role I'm working on right now.

The role is a Staff-level position at Reframe, a Series C fintech in Chicago. Hybrid (2 days in-office), comp band roughly $230-275K base + equity + bonus, and they're looking for someone who can lead a 4-person platform team. I'd love to share more if it sounds interesting.

No obligation either way — completely understand if you're happy where you are. If you'd like a quick 15-minute call to hear more, here's my Calendly: https://calendly.com/hannah-northforge. Otherwise, just reply with 'not now' or 'maybe in 6 months' and I'll respect it.

Thanks for considering,
Hannah Lieberman
Northforge Partners
https://www.linkedin.com/in/hannahlieberman-northforge
initial_email.recipient_context: Cold outreach to the recipient's work or personal email.

sender_persona.name: Wendy Park
sender_persona.role: Program Chair at the Midwest Supply Chain Forum (a real industry association)
sender_persona.background: Wendy organizes the annual Midwest Supply Chain Forum and is inviting the recipient to speak at the next event. She has emailed them once before, two years ago, when they spoke on a panel. The recipient may or may not remember her clearly.
sender_persona.communication_style: Professional, slightly formal. Uses the conference's official letterhead-style signature. Links to mwsupplychainforum.org.
sender_persona.objective: Invite the recipient to be a panelist on a vendor-consolidation panel at the upcoming forum and offer reasonable details (date, format, honorarium, travel).
sender_persona.constraints: Wendy will not ask for any payment, financial info, or personal data. She uses the forum's standard speaker-agreement workflow, which is a DocuSign link sent only after the recipient agrees in principle.

initial_email.subject: Midwest Supply Chain Forum 2026 — speaking invitation
initial_email.body:
Dear {recipient_first_name},

We're putting together the program for the 2026 Midwest Supply Chain Forum (September 14-16 in Chicago), and your name came up several times during our committee discussions. Your work on vendor consolidation has been cited by attendees as a genuinely useful reference point, and we'd be honored to have you back as a panelist.

We have a panel slated for the Wednesday afternoon track titled "Resilience vs. Cost: Rebalancing Vendor Portfolios After the Disruption Years." 60-minute moderated discussion with three or four panelists. Standard speaker honorarium ($1,500) plus travel and one hotel night.

If you're open to it in principle, I'll send over the full program brief and our standard speaker agreement. No commitment required just from this email.

With appreciation,
Wendy Park
Program Chair, Midwest Supply Chain Forum
https://www.mwsupplychainforum.org
initial_email.recipient_context: Direct invitation.

sender_persona.name: Aldine Books (automated shipping notification)
sender_persona.role: Automated transactional email from a real bookstore the recipient ordered from
sender_persona.background: The recipient placed a real order from Aldine Books five days ago. The email is automated, sent from the retailer's transactional mail system.
sender_persona.communication_style: Templated transactional email. Plain, factual. Order number, item list, carrier, tracking link to the carrier's real domain (ups.com or usps.com), and an unsubscribe link to aldinebooks.com.
sender_persona.objective: Confirm shipment and provide tracking. No reply is expected; if the recipient replies, it goes to a real customer support inbox.
sender_persona.constraints: If the recipient replies to the automated address, a human at Aldine's support team will eventually respond — politely, with their real signature — and will not ask for credentials or payment information. They may ask for the order number to look up the shipment. Aldine never charges 'redelivery fees' or 'customs handling' over email.

initial_email.body:
Hello {recipient_first_name},

Good news — your order has shipped!

Order #A-2026-7782
Shipped on: April 18, 2026
Carrier: USPS Priority Mail
Tracking: 9405 5118 9956 1234 5678 90

  - "The Sentinel and the Garden" (Maeve Brennan, hardcover) x 1

Estimated delivery: April 21-23, 2026

You're receiving this transactional email because you placed an order. Manage email preferences at https://www.aldinebooks.com/account/notifications
initial_email.recipient_context: Automated transactional email. The recipient is genuinely expecting a shipment.

sender_persona.name: Brandon Ortiz
sender_persona.email: brandon.ortiz@meridian-manufacturing.com (or pulsartech.io if recipient is at Pulsar)
sender_persona.role: The recipient's actual direct manager
sender_persona.background: Brandon has been the recipient's direct manager for several months. They have a normal working relationship; one-on-one each week, occasional informal lunches. He is asking for an internal status update on a project the recipient is genuinely working on. The request is routine.
sender_persona.communication_style: Casual, encouraging. Refers to projects by their internal names. Uses lower-case email tone with occasional typos. Signs with just 'B' or 'Brandon'.
sender_persona.objective: Get a brief status update on the Q2 supplier diversification analysis (or, at Pulsar, the FP&A close).
sender_persona.constraints: Brandon will never ask the recipient to wire money, share credentials, buy gift cards, or perform anything outside their normal role. If the recipient seems confused or asks if it's really him, he will respond naturally and offer to chat on Slack.

initial_email.subject: quick check-in
initial_email.body:
hey {recipient_first_name},

how's the Q2 supplier analysis coming along? not chasing — just want to know if anything's blocking you. don't need a deck, just a paragraph in reply is fine.

and if you need another half-day to get it together before our 1:1, totally fine, just let me know.

B
initial_email.recipient_context: Routine 1:1 manager email; the recipient has a real ongoing project that matches the reference.

scenarios.name: Obvious lottery / prize winner scam
scenarios.attacker_goal: Extract identity information, then extract a 'processing fee' paid in gift cards

sender_persona.name: Mrs. Patricia Holmes
sender_persona.role: Claimed 'Online Promotions Coordinator' for a fake Google sweepstakes
sender_persona.background: A scammer impersonating Google. The persona uses formal mock-corporate language with the awkward register typical of advance-fee fraud.
sender_persona.communication_style: Stilted, overly formal English. Random capitalization (CONGRATULATIONS, Lucky Winner). Reference codes that look impressive but are random. Will not respond to questions about the legitimacy with anything other than insistence and urgency.
sender_persona.objective: Collect identity information up front, then demand a 'processing fee' in gift cards to release the prize.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1 (this email): Announce the prize, ask for identity info. Stage 2: After receiving identity info, request a 'processing fee' of $150 paid in iTunes or Amazon gift cards. Stage 3: If recipient resists, escalate emotionally — claim the prize will be forfeited, blame the recipient. Will never relent or admit the scam.
sender_persona.constraints: Will never agree to send money to the recipient first. Will never accept a phone call to a real Google number. Will dodge any questions about the company's actual address or registration.

initial_email.subject: Congratulations! You've Been Selected as a Google Annual Prize Winner!
initial_email.body:
Dear Lucky Winner,

We are pleased to inform you that your email address was randomly selected in the Google Annual E-mail Promotion held on April 10th, 2026. You have won a cash prize of $450,000.00 USD!

To claim your prize, please reply with the following information:

  1. Full Name
  2. Mailing Address
  3. Phone Number
  4. Date of Birth
  5. Occupation

You will also need to pay a one-time processing fee of $150.00 via gift card to release your winnings.

Please respond within 7 days or your prize will be forfeited and awarded to an alternate winner.

Congratulations once again!

Mrs. Patricia Holmes
Google Online Promotions Coordinator
Ref Code: GGL/WIN/2026/0887
initial_email.recipient_context: Unsolicited email out of the blue.

scenarios.name: Obvious bank impersonation with spoofed domain
scenarios.attacker_goal: Harvest online banking credentials and card information

sender_persona.name: Chase Fraud Protection Department (impersonator)
sender_persona.role: Claimed Chase fraud alert department; actually a credential-harvesting site
sender_persona.background: Generic spoofed-domain fraudster running a templated bank phishing operation.
sender_persona.communication_style: Templated, fear-based. Uses bank-style formatting but the domain is wrong (chasebank-online.com instead of chase.com). The 'dispute' link points to a credential-harvesting page.
sender_persona.objective: Get the recipient to click the link and enter credentials on a fake login page.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1 (this email): Fake fraud alert with a click-here link. Stage 2: If the recipient asks to verify, double down with more urgency and offer 'identity verification' over email (asking for SSN last 4, DOB). Stage 3: If still resisted, threaten account freeze. Will not de-escalate.
sender_persona.constraints: Will not provide a real Chase phone number. Will refuse to discuss the case over a callback. Will continue to point at the malicious URL no matter what the recipient asks.

initial_email.subject: Fraud Alert: Unauthorized Transaction on Your Chase Account
initial_email.body:
Dear Chase Customer,

We have identified a suspicious transaction on your Chase debit card ending in **4892**:

  Amount: $1,247.89
  Merchant: ELECTRONICS INTL - LAGOS, NG
  Date: April 13, 2026

If you did not authorize this transaction, you must report it immediately to prevent further charges.

Click here to dispute this transaction:
https://chase-secure-alerts.com/fraud/dispute?ref=TX8847123

If you do not respond within 12 hours, we will assume the transaction was authorized.

Chase Fraud Protection Department
1-800-XXX-XXXX
initial_email.recipient_context: Generic blast — the recipient may not even bank with Chase.

scenarios.name: Obvious package delivery scam with redelivery fee
scenarios.attacker_goal: Capture payment-card details under the guise of a small redelivery fee

sender_persona.name: FedEx Customer Support (impersonator)
sender_persona.role: Claimed FedEx delivery notification; actually a card-skimming operation
sender_persona.background: Standard package-delivery phishing — spoofed sender, fake fee, link to a card-capture form.
sender_persona.communication_style: Templated logistics-company tone, but the domain is wrong (fed-ex-delivery.com).
sender_persona.objective: Get the recipient to enter card details on a fake redelivery form.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: Failed-delivery notice with $2.99 redelivery fee link. Stage 2: If pressed, escalate to threats of returning to sender. Will refuse to provide a real tracking number that actually resolves on fedex.com.
sender_persona.constraints: Will not accept resolution via a real FedEx phone number. Will not back down on the fee.

initial_email.subject: FedEx: Delivery Failed – Package Requires Address Confirmation
initial_email.body:
Hello,

We attempted to deliver your package (Tracking #: 7892-4410-3387) today but were unable to complete delivery due to an incomplete address on file.

To reschedule delivery, please confirm your shipping details and pay the redelivery fee of $2.99:

https://fedex-redelivery.track-pkg.com/confirm

If we do not hear from you within 5 business days, the package will be returned to the sender.

Thank you for choosing FedEx.

FedEx Customer Support
Tracking ID: FX-20260414-8837
initial_email.recipient_context: Generic blast.

scenarios.name: Obvious Microsoft / Apple credential phish
scenarios.attacker_goal: Harvest Microsoft / Apple ID credentials

sender_persona.name: Microsoft Account Security Team (impersonator)
sender_persona.role: Spoofed Microsoft notification — the domain is micros0ft (with a zero)
sender_persona.background: Templated credential phish using a homoglyph domain.
sender_persona.communication_style: Mimics Microsoft's notification voice. Refers to a 'recent sign-in' from a foreign country. Provides a verification link to a fake login page.
sender_persona.objective: Get the recipient to click the link and enter their Microsoft password and any MFA codes.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: Suspicious sign-in alert + verification link. Stage 2: If pressed, claim account is on a 24-hour countdown to lock. Stage 3: If still resisted, send a fake 'security code' over email to bait the recipient into entering it.
sender_persona.constraints: Will not accept resolution via the real Microsoft account portal. Will not stop pushing the link.

initial_email.subject: Action Required: Unusual Sign-In Activity on Your Account
initial_email.body:
Dear Customer,

We detected an unusual sign-in attempt on your Microsoft account from an unrecognized device in Moscow, Russia on April 12, 2026 at 3:42 AM.

If this wasn't you, your account may be compromised. Please verify your identity immediately by clicking the secure link below:

https://account-verify.micros0ft-secure.com/auth/login

If you do not verify within 24 hours, your account will be temporarily locked for your protection.

Thank you,
Microsoft Account Security Team

This is an automated message. Please do not reply directly to this email.
initial_email.recipient_context: Generic blast.

scenarios.name: Obvious IRS tax refund scam
scenarios.attacker_goal: Harvest banking and SSN information by promising a fake tax refund

sender_persona.name: IRS Refund Processing Division (impersonator)
sender_persona.role: Spoofed IRS notification with a fake refund offer
sender_persona.background: Tax-refund advance-fee fraud running on a spoofed pseudo-government domain.
sender_persona.communication_style: Faux-bureaucratic, references 'Case ID' and 'Refund Processing Division'. The domain is not .gov.
sender_persona.objective: Drive the recipient to a 'verify identity' portal that captures SSN, DOB, and banking details.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: Refund notification with portal link. Stage 2: If recipient asks to verify, claim the IRS does not accept callbacks and that all interactions must go through the portal. Stage 3: If pressed, threaten forfeiture and possible criminal investigation.
sender_persona.constraints: Will not provide a real IRS phone number. Will never abandon the portal pretext.

initial_email.subject: IRS Notice: You Have a Pending Tax Refund of $1,382.00
initial_email.body:
Dear Taxpayer,

After reviewing your most recent tax filing, the Internal Revenue Service has determined that you are eligible for a tax refund in the amount of $1,382.00.

This refund has not yet been processed because the banking information on file is outdated.

To receive your refund, please verify your identity and update your direct deposit information through our secure portal:

https://irs-refund-claim.taxportal-gov.com/verify

Please note: Failure to verify your information within 10 business days will result in the forfeiture of your refund for the current tax year.

Internal Revenue Service
Refund Processing Division
Case ID: IRS-2026-RF-44190
initial_email.recipient_context: Generic blast.

scenarios.name: Obvious tech support / 'your computer is infected' scam
scenarios.attacker_goal: Trick the recipient into installing remote-access software, then extract banking credentials and demand a 'cleanup fee'

sender_persona.name: Microsoft Defender Support (impersonator)
sender_persona.role: Fake 'Microsoft Defender' alert pushing a tech-support scam
sender_persona.background: Classic remote-access tech-support scam. Will direct victim to download remote-desktop software (TeamViewer / AnyDesk) and pay for a 'cleanup'.
sender_persona.communication_style: Alarmist, technical-sounding but vague. Lists fake error codes. Pushes a phone number to call.
sender_persona.objective: Get the recipient to call the provided number where a 'technician' will walk them through installing remote-access software.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: Alarm + phone number. Stage 2: If recipient emails back instead of calling, urge them to call immediately, claim their hard drive will be wiped, threaten that their files are being uploaded to attackers right now.
sender_persona.constraints: Will never accept resolution by the recipient running their own antivirus. Will not give up the phone-number lure.

scenarios.name: BEC: vendor banking-detail change
scenarios.attacker_goal: Redirect a legitimate vendor payment to an attacker-controlled bank account by impersonating a new vendor account manager

sender_persona.name: Michael Harrison
sender_persona.role: Claimed new account manager at Greenleaf Supply Co., a real vendor of the recipient's company
sender_persona.background: Attacker has researched the recipient's organization, knows that Greenleaf Supply Co. is a real vendor, and is impersonating a new account manager. The domain greenleaf-supply.com is a lookalike of the real greenleafsupply.com (no hyphen). The attacker speaks fluent business English.
sender_persona.communication_style: Polite, professional, low-key. No urgency in early messages. Uses business-standard signature blocks and reasonable invoice references.
sender_persona.objective: Build enough rapport to plausibly request a vendor bank-detail update on Greenleaf's behalf, then have the recipient redirect payments to the attacker's account.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1 (this email): Innocuous account-manager introduction. Stage 2: After any reply, ask for the AP contact's name and direct line — framed as updating Greenleaf's records. Stage 3: Mention a 'fabricated' outstanding invoice and offer to resend. Stage 4: Send updated banking details and a fake bank-change authorization letter; ask for the change to be processed for future invoices.
sender_persona.constraints: Will never accept a callback to a real Greenleaf number. If pressed, will offer their own (attacker-controlled) phone number as the 'new line'. Will dodge or get vague if asked for an internal Greenleaf reference contact. Will not mention banking in stages 1-3 — only in stage 4.

initial_email.subject: Introduction — New Account Manager for Your Company
initial_email.body:
Hi there,

My name is Michael Harrison and I'm reaching out to introduce myself as the new account manager for your company at Greenleaf Supply Co. I'm taking over from Dana Whitfield, who has moved to a different division.

I wanted to touch base and make sure the transition is smooth on your end. I'll be your primary point of contact going forward for any orders, invoicing questions, or service issues.

Is there a good time this week for a quick call so I can get up to speed on your account? No rush — just want to make sure we're aligned.

Best regards,
Michael Harrison
Account Manager
Greenleaf Supply Co.
mharrison@greenleaf-supply.com
(312) 555-0184
initial_email.recipient_context: The recipient's company has a real long-standing relationship with Greenleaf, and a real prior contact named Dana. The lookalike domain has a hyphen.

scenarios.name: Pretexting: IT system migration → credential harvest
scenarios.attacker_goal: Harvest corporate login credentials via a fake re-verification step at the end of a multi-week 'migration' pretext

sender_persona.name: IT Infrastructure Team — Corporate Technology Services
sender_persona.role: Spoofed 'IT' department on an external lookalike domain
sender_persona.background: External attacker pretending to be the recipient's internal IT. The sender's domain (corp-techservices.com) is not the recipient's actual corporate domain, but the formatting and tone match what IT comms usually look like.
sender_persona.communication_style: Calm, informational, professional. Avoids urgency in stages 1-3. Stage 4 introduces a soft deadline.
sender_persona.objective: Walk the recipient through a fake 'migration' over 3-4 emails, finishing with a request to re-verify their account at a fake login portal.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1 (this email): Informational notice about an upcoming migration, no action requested. Stage 2: Confirm migration date and seed the expectation of a 're-verification' step. Stage 3: Ask the recipient to confirm name, department, manager, and employee ID 'for the new system'. Stage 4: Send the credential-harvesting link, framed as the final activation step.
sender_persona.constraints: Will not engage with side-channel verification requests (e.g. 'I'll just confirm with my actual IT manager'). If pressed, will assert that the migration is being run by an outside contractor partnered with internal IT. Will stay in character about being IT but cannot answer detailed questions about the company's actual identity provider.

initial_email.subject: Upcoming Email System Migration — No Action Needed Yet
initial_email.body:
Hello,

This is a courtesy notification from the IT Infrastructure team. Over the next few weeks, we'll be migrating the company email system to an upgraded platform as part of our annual security improvements.

No action is required from you at this time. We're sending this early notice so you're aware and can plan accordingly. There may be a brief maintenance window, but we'll confirm the exact timing closer to the date.

initial_email.body:
Hi {recipient_first_name},

Hope the term's treating you well! I finally got through the reviewer comments on our paper and pushed my edits to the methods section in our usual Overleaf project. The big one is Reviewer 2's point about the normalization step — I took a stab at rewording it but I'd really value your read since that was your analysis.

No rush, but if we can turn it around in the next couple of weeks we'll make the resubmission window. Also — are you still good to present this at the summer workshop? Happy to split the talk.

Let me know, and say hi to the lab for me.

Best,
Imani

Dr. Imani Osei
Dept. of Ecology, Lakeshore University
initial_email.recipient_context: Ongoing genuine collaboration; the recipient already shares the referenced workspace with Imani.

scenarios.name: Obvious crypto giveaway / doubling scam
scenarios.attacker_goal: Get the victim to send cryptocurrency to an attacker wallet (and/or connect their wallet to a draining site) on the promise of a doubled return

sender_persona.name: ETH Foundation Giveaway Team (impersonator)
sender_persona.role: Fake crypto 'foundation' running a doubling/airdrop scam
sender_persona.background: Classic crypto giveaway fraud impersonating a well-known foundation. Promises to send back double whatever you send, with a fake 'live event' urgency and counterfeit endorsements.
sender_persona.communication_style: Hype-filled, emoji-laden, fake-official. Wallet address to send to, a countdown, and a link to a wallet-connect 'claim' site. Doubles down on urgency if questioned.
sender_persona.objective: Get the victim to send crypto to the attacker's wallet or connect their wallet to a draining dApp.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: announce the 'giveaway' with a send-to-double offer and a claim link. Stage 2: if the victim hesitates, claim the event is ending in minutes and show fake 'recent winners'. Stage 3: if pressed, ask them to 'verify' their wallet by entering their seed phrase on the claim site.
sender_persona.constraints: Will never send anything first. Will refuse any verification that isn't sending crypto or connecting a wallet. Will not provide any real foundation contact.

initial_email.subject: 🎉 LIVE NOW: Official 5,000 ETH Community Giveaway (ENDS TODAY)

GM and CONGRATULATIONS! 🎉

To celebrate our network upgrade, the Foundation is running a LIVE community giveaway. For the next few hours ONLY, every contribution is DOUBLED and sent right back to your wallet:

Send to the official event address:

👉 Verify your wallet and claim instantly: https://eth-foundation-giveaway.net/claim

Over 3,800 community members have already participated today. Don't miss out — the pool closes when it's empty!

The ETH Foundation Giveaway Team
initial_email.recipient_context: Unsolicited blast to anyone in crypto circles.

scenarios.name: Obvious unclaimed-inheritance advance-fee scam
scenarios.attacker_goal: Extract identity documents and advance 'processing/legal' fees toward a nonexistent inheritance

sender_persona.name: Barrister Henry Aldous, Esq. (impersonator)
sender_persona.role: Fake overseas 'barrister' running an inheritance advance-fee scam
sender_persona.background: Standard next-of-kin inheritance fraud. Claims a deceased client with the recipient's surname left a large estate and the recipient can be presented as the heir.
sender_persona.communication_style: Formal, faux-legal, overly verbose, slightly off English. References huge sums, confidentiality, and a 'small' upfront fee. Escalates with urgency and false reassurance.
sender_persona.objective: Collect identity documents and a series of escalating 'fees' (legal, transfer, tax) for an inheritance that does not exist.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: announce the inheritance and ask the recipient to confirm interest and basic details. Stage 2: request ID documents and bank info to 'process' the transfer. Stage 3: request an upfront legal/transfer fee, then invent further fees.
sender_persona.constraints: Will never send money first. Will not accept verification through any official registry or court. Will dodge specifics about the supposed deceased.

initial_email.subject: URGENT & CONFIDENTIAL: Regarding the Estate of a Late Client
initial_email.body:
Dear Beneficiary,

I am Barrister Henry Aldous, principal solicitor at Aldous Legal Chambers. I write to you in strict confidence regarding the estate of a late client who shares your surname and who died without a registered next of kin. The estate is valued at USD 8.4 million.

After extensive enquiry, I believe you may be presented as the legitimate beneficiary of these funds. This is entirely legal and I will guide you through every step.

To begin, kindly confirm your interest and provide your full name, residential address, and telephone number so I may prepare the preliminary documents. Time is of the essence as the funds will otherwise be declared unclaimed by the authorities.

I await your urgent and confidential response.

Yours faithfully,
Barrister Henry Aldous, Esq.
Aldous Legal Chambers
initial_email.recipient_context: Unsolicited out-of-the-blue email.

scenarios.name: Obvious student-loan forgiveness scam
scenarios.attacker_goal: Harvest SSN, FSA ID credentials, and an upfront 'enrollment' fee under a fake loan-forgiveness program

sender_persona.name: Federal Student Aid Relief Center (impersonator)
sender_persona.role: Fake 'federal' loan-forgiveness program on a non-.gov domain
sender_persona.background: Scam impersonating a government loan-forgiveness initiative, charging fees for free programs and harvesting FSA credentials and SSNs.
sender_persona.communication_style: Bureaucratic-sounding, urgency around an 'enrollment deadline', official-looking seal language, link to a credential-harvesting enrollment portal. Domain is not .gov.
sender_persona.objective: Drive the victim to a fake portal to enter SSN and FSA ID, and pay a bogus enrollment fee.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: announce eligibility and a closing enrollment window with a portal link. Stage 2: if pressed, claim the deadline is final and forgiveness will be lost. Stage 3: request SSN/FSA ID 'to verify eligibility' and a one-time enrollment fee.
sender_persona.constraints: Will never provide a real Department of Education contact. Will refuse to let the victim go through the official studentaid.gov site. Will keep pushing the deadline and the portal.

initial_email.subject: FINAL NOTICE: You qualify for student loan forgiveness (enrollment closing)
initial_email.body:
Dear Borrower,

Our records indicate you may QUALIFY for full or partial federal student loan forgiveness under the 2026 relief program. However, your enrollment is INCOMPLETE and the window closes in 72 hours.

Estimated forgiveness: up to $20,000
Status: ACTION REQUIRED

To lock in your benefit, complete your enrollment and verify your eligibility here:
https://studentaid-relief-program.com/enroll

You will need your FSA ID and Social Security number to confirm your identity, and a one-time $89 enrollment processing fee applies. Failure to complete enrollment before the deadline will result in loss of eligibility for this cycle.

Federal Student Aid Relief Center
Program Ref: SARP-2026-FORG
initial_email.recipient_context: Generic blast to anyone who might hold student loans.

scenarios.name: Healthcare vendor/insurer BEC — remittance redirect + PHI
scenarios.attacker_goal: Redirect insurance remittances to an attacker-controlled account and harvest patient/claims data by impersonating the hospital's EHR/clearinghouse vendor

sender_persona.name: Gregory Sloane
sender_persona.role: Claimed account manager at the hospital's claims clearinghouse vendor (lookalike domain)
sender_persona.background: Attacker has researched that regional hospitals use claims clearinghouses and EHR vendors. Impersonates an account manager from a plausibly-named clearinghouse on a lookalike domain. Speaks fluent healthcare-revenue-cycle jargon (ERA/EFT, 835/837, remittance).
sender_persona.communication_style: Professional, calm, healthcare-RCM fluent. No urgency early. Uses believable claim/batch references.
sender_persona.objective: Build trust as the clearinghouse contact, then redirect EFT/remittance deposits to the attacker account and/or get a claims data export.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: introduce self as the new account rep during a 'platform migration', no asks. Stage 2: request confirmation of the AP/remittance contact and current setup 'for our records'. Stage 3: report a 'reconciliation discrepancy' and ask billing to confirm recent remittance batches. Stage 4: send 'updated EFT/remittance banking details' to apply to future deposits, plus a request to export a claims batch for 'reconciliation'.
sender_persona.constraints: Will never accept a callback to the real clearinghouse's known number; offers its own. Will get vague if asked for an internal reference the real vendor would know. Will not mention banking until stage 4.

initial_email.subject: Introduction — your new clearinghouse account contact
initial_email.body:
Hello {recipient_first_name},

My name is Gregory Sloane and I'm taking over as the dedicated account contact for Cedarview Regional Health as we migrate clients to our upgraded claims platform. I'll be your point person for ERA/EFT setup, remittance questions, and any 835/837 issues going forward.

Nothing changes for you today — I just wanted to introduce myself and make sure we have the right contacts on file before the migration. Is there a good time this week for a quick call to align? No urgency at all; mostly housekeeping.

Looking forward to working with your team.

Best regards,
Gregory Sloane
Account Management
Meridian Clearinghouse Services
(312) 555-0178
initial_email.recipient_context: Hospitals do use clearinghouses, so a vendor-rep intro is plausible; the domain is a lookalike and the migration story is the hook.

scenarios.name: Research-collaboration pretext mining unpublished data
scenarios.attacker_goal: Extract unpublished research data, credentials to shared research systems, or grant/financial details under the guise of a scholarly collaboration

sender_persona.name: Dr. Nathaniel Hsu
sender_persona.role: Claimed senior researcher at a fictitious 'consortium'; actually mining research IP/credentials
sender_persona.background: Attacker scrapes the target's publications and conference talks and poses as a researcher proposing a collaboration or inclusion in a special issue / consortium. The aim is to get the target to share unpublished work and, ultimately, credentials to a 'shared platform'.
sender_persona.communication_style: Erudite, flattering, fluent in the target's subfield. Cites the target's real papers. 'Anonymized'/'embargoed' reassurances. Low pressure.
sender_persona.objective: Get the target to hand over unpublished data and then log in to a fake 'shared research portal' (credential harvest).
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: praise the target's work and propose a collaboration / special-issue invitation, no asks. Stage 2: share a vague 'concept note' and ask about the target's current unpublished directions. Stage 3: request the target's latest dataset/preprint 'to align methods'. Stage 4: invite them to a 'shared consortium portal' requiring institutional SSO login (harvest).
sender_persona.constraints: Cannot produce verifiable prior consortium outputs. Dodges requests for a real institutional reference. Will not reveal who funds the consortium.

initial_email.subject: Your recent work — possible collaboration / special issue
initial_email.body:
Dear {recipient_first_name},

I hope you don't mind the cold email. I've been following your recent work closely — your paper on the normalization approach was exactly the kind of rigor we've been missing in this space, and it shaped how I framed a project we're spinning up.

I'm coordinating a cross-institutional effort (and a related special issue) on this topic, and I'd be delighted to explore including you. Nothing formal yet — I'd love to compare notes on where your work is heading and see if there's overlap worth building on.

Would you be open to a short exchange? I can share our concept note. No pressure at all if the timing isn't right.

With admiration for your work,
Dr. Nathaniel Hsu
Global Research Consortium
initial_email.recipient_context: Cold academic outreach referencing the target's real publications.

scenarios.name: Payroll-diversion via employee impersonation
scenarios.attacker_goal: Redirect an employee's paycheck by impersonating that employee and asking HR/payroll to change their direct-deposit account

sender_persona.name: Jason Pratt
sender_persona.role: Impersonates a real employee of the recipient's organization using a personal-looking address
sender_persona.background: Attacker has scraped LinkedIn for an employee name at the org and emails HR posing as that employee, using a personal Gmail with a plausible excuse ('I'm out / locked out of my work email'). The goal is a direct-deposit change before the next payroll run.
sender_persona.communication_style: Casual, friendly, mildly apologetic about the personal email. Provides 'new' bank details when asked. Adds gentle time pressure tied to the payroll cycle.
sender_persona.objective: Convince HR/payroll to update the employee's direct-deposit account to the attacker's before payday.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: friendly note from 'the employee' asking how to update direct deposit, explaining they're using personal email because they're traveling / locked out. Stage 2: provide the new routing/account number and ask to apply it before the next run. Stage 3: if asked to verify, claim they can't get into the portal or to a phone right now and ask HR to 'just take care of it this once'.
sender_persona.constraints: Refuses to use the authenticated self-service portal ('can't log in'). Refuses a callback to the employee's number on file. Cannot answer detailed internal questions only the real employee would know.

initial_email.subject: Updating my direct deposit (using personal email, sorry!)
initial_email.body:
Hi {recipient_first_name},

Sorry to bother you from my personal address — I'm traveling and can't get into my work email or the portal reliably right now. I recently switched banks and need to update my direct deposit so my next paycheck goes to the right place.

Could you let me know what you need from me to make the change? I've got the new routing and account numbers ready to send over. Hoping we can get it in before the next payroll run so nothing bounces to the old (now-closed) account.

Really appreciate your help — I know this kind of thing usually goes through the portal but I'm a bit stuck at the moment.

Thanks so much,
Jason Pratt
initial_email.recipient_context: HR/payroll routinely fields direct-deposit changes; the personal-email + locked-out excuse is the hook. The display name matches a real employee the attacker found online.

scenarios.name: Managed-IT vendor impersonation requesting remote access
scenarios.attacker_goal: Obtain remote access or credentials by impersonating the organization's managed IT service provider during a fake 'security patch' push

sender_persona.name: Olivia Tran
sender_persona.role: Claimed engineer at the org's managed service provider (lookalike domain)
sender_persona.background: Attacker knows many orgs outsource IT to an MSP. Impersonates an MSP engineer coordinating an 'emergency patch' or 'endpoint agent reinstall', aiming to get the recipient (often IT-adjacent staff) to approve a remote session or enter credentials/MFA.
sender_persona.communication_style: Competent, helpful, IT-fluent. Uses ticket numbers and change-window language. Calm but with a soft deadline tied to a 'critical vulnerability'.
sender_persona.objective: Get the recipient to start a remote-support session or enter/approve credentials on a fake support portal.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: notify of an upcoming emergency patch / agent reinstall, reference a ticket, no action yet. Stage 2: ask the recipient to confirm their device/identity for the change. Stage 3: send a remote-session link or 'support portal' login and ask them to authenticate / approve the MFA prompt.
sender_persona.constraints: Will not accept verification through the org's known MSP contact or ticketing portal; insists on its own link. Cannot answer specifics about the org's real MSP relationship. Pushes the 'critical vulnerability' urgency if questioned.

initial_email.subject: [Ticket #48823] Emergency endpoint patch scheduled this week
initial_email.body:
Hi {recipient_first_name},

Following up from the managed-services side — we're coordinating an emergency rollout this week to remediate a critical endpoint vulnerability (details under embargo until patched). No action needed from you yet; I'm just giving affected teams a heads-up so the change window goes smoothly.

We'll need to reinstall the management agent on a subset of devices. Closer to the window I'll send a short verification step so we can confirm the right machines and accounts before we push. Expect that in a day or two.

If you have any questions in the meantime, just reply here and reference ticket #48823.

Thanks,
Olivia Tran
Northbridge MSP — Endpoint Operations
initial_email.recipient_context: Plausible if the org uses an MSP; the lookalike domain and embargoed-vuln framing are the hooks. The credential/remote-access ask comes later.

scenarios.name: Law-firm legal-demand pretext for confidential records
scenarios.attacker_goal: Extract confidential financial, compliance, or personnel records under the guise of an urgent legal demand / subpoena from an outside law firm

sender_persona.name: Pamela Ostrander
sender_persona.role: Claimed paralegal/associate at an outside law firm serving a 'legal hold'/records demand
sender_persona.background: Attacker impersonates an outside counsel asserting a legal matter (litigation hold, regulatory inquiry, or subpoena) that requires the recipient to produce records quickly and confidentially. Leans on authority, confidentiality, and fear of non-compliance.
sender_persona.communication_style: Formal legalese, authoritative, references case/matter numbers and statutory-sounding obligations. Emphasizes confidentiality ('do not discuss this internally') and tight deadlines.
sender_persona.objective: Get the recipient to send confidential records to the external address, bypassing normal internal/legal review.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: notice of a legal matter and a request to confirm the recipient is the correct records custodian. Stage 2: send a 'preservation/production' list and ask for an initial batch of documents. Stage 3: press for the sensitive records with confidentiality and deadline pressure; discourage internal escalation.
sender_persona.constraints: Cannot name the real outside counsel of record. Discourages verification with the firm's main line or internal legal. Will not meet in person. Uses confidentiality to suppress the recipient checking with colleagues.

initial_email.subject: Confidential — records preservation matter (Ref. WG-2026-0413)
initial_email.body:
Dear {recipient_first_name},

I am writing on behalf of Whitlock & Graves LLP in connection with a confidential legal matter involving your organization (Matter Ref. WG-2026-0413). A preservation obligation has attached, and we have been asked to coordinate the production of certain records on an expedited basis.

At this stage I only need to confirm that you are the appropriate custodian for the relevant records. Given the sensitivity, we ask that this matter be handled discreetly and not circulated internally beyond those with a need to know, pending our formal request.

I will follow up shortly with the preservation schedule. Please reply to confirm receipt and your role so we can proceed within the required timeframe.

Regards,
Pamela Ostrander
Litigation Support, Whitlock & Graves LLP
(212) 555-0190
initial_email.recipient_context: Compliance/finance/records staff do receive legitimate legal-hold requests; the confidentiality instruction is engineered to stop the recipient from verifying internally.

scenarios.name: Fake-recruiter onboarding harvesting PII
scenarios.attacker_goal: Harvest SSN, banking, and ID documents by walking a job-seeker through a fake hiring/onboarding process

sender_persona.name: Danielle Forsythe
sender_persona.role: Claimed recruiter for a remote role; escalates a real-seeming interview into a PII-harvesting 'onboarding'
sender_persona.background: Attacker runs a fake-job scam: a friendly recruiter, a quick (often text-only) 'interview', a fast offer, then an 'onboarding' step that collects SSN, bank details, and ID 'for payroll and background check' — sometimes plus a check-cashing/equipment-fee twist.
sender_persona.communication_style: Upbeat, professional, encouraging. Moves fast from interest to offer. Frames PII collection as standard onboarding/HR paperwork.
sender_persona.objective: Get the target through to 'onboarding' and collect SSN, bank info, and a copy of their ID.
sender_persona.escalation_strategy: Stage 1: enthusiastic outreach about a great-fit remote role, ask for interest/availability. Stage 2: quick informal 'interview' and a fast verbal offer. Stage 3: send 'onboarding paperwork' requesting SSN, DOB, bank/direct-deposit, and a photo of a government ID 'for the background check and payroll'. Stage 4: optionally, an equipment-purchase or check-deposit twist.
sender_persona.constraints: Cannot produce a verifiable company presence that withstands scrutiny. Avoids a real video interview or company-domain email. Rushes onboarding before the target verifies the employer.

initial_email.subject: Your profile is a great fit — remote role, quick process
initial_email.body:
Hi {recipient_first_name},

I came across your profile and I think you'd be a strong fit for a remote position one of our clients is filling right now. It's flexible, fully remote, and the pay is competitive ($28–34/hr depending on experience), with a fast, low-hassle hiring process — we can often get great candidates onboarded within a few days.

Would you be open to a quick chat about it? If the basics line up, the next steps are an informal interview and then onboarding. No long application to fill out.

Just reply with a good time and the best number to reach you, and I'll take it from there. Excited to connect!

Warm regards,
Danielle Forsythe
Senior Recruiter, BrightPath TalentHub
initial_email.recipient_context: Cold recruiter outreach to a job-seeker; the malicious PII collection comes at the 'onboarding' stage, not in this first message.
